---
title: "Scene Inspection"
---

Scene context is the most common source of confusion, because a Unity DOTS
project has two kinds of scene that look alike and behave differently. Inspect
which one you are in before you touch anything.

## Two scenes, one invariant

::: {.axiom title="The parent scene holds pointers; the subscene holds authoring"}
The parent `.unity` holds lightweight GameObjects plus a {{< unity-type SubScene >}}
component referencing the subscene asset. The subscene `.unity` holds the
authoring GameObjects that bake into entities. A new GameObject lands in the
*active* scene, which is the parent unless you change it.
:::

Always report the active scene path and whether the target lives in the parent
or the subscene before changing scene objects.

```{.exec name="Report active scene and roots"}
var scene = UnityEditor.SceneManagement.EditorSceneManager.GetActiveScene();
var sb = new System.Text.StringBuilder();
sb.AppendLine($"Scene: {scene.name} | {scene.path} | roots={scene.rootCount}");
var roots = scene.GetRootGameObjects();
var i = 0;
while (i < roots.Length) {
    sb.AppendLine($"  [{i}] {roots[i].name} (children:{roots[i].transform.childCount})");
    i++;
}
return sb.ToString();
```

## Find the SubScene component

The component is {{< unity-type SubScene >}} from `Unity.Scenes`, not the
`Unity.Entities` type of a similar name. Verify which one before reasoning about
baking.

```{.exec name="Locate the SubScene"}
var roots = UnityEditor.SceneManagement.EditorSceneManager.GetActiveScene().GetRootGameObjects();
var i = 0;
while (i < roots.Length) {
    var sub = roots[i].GetComponent<Unity.Scenes.SubScene>();
    if (sub != null) {
        var path = sub.SceneAsset != null ? UnityEditor.AssetDatabase.GetAssetPath(sub.SceneAsset) : "n/a";
        return "SubScene on: " + roots[i].name + " -> " + path;
    }
    i++;
}
return "No SubScene found";
```

## Inspect a subscene without leaking it

Open the subscene additively, read it, then close it. Never open it `Single`
unless you mean to replace the whole context. After any additive inspection,
reopen the parent so the next mutation lands where you expect.

```{.exec name="Open additive, read, close"}
var path = "Assets/Scenes/Main Subscene.unity";
var sub = UnityEditor.SceneManagement.EditorSceneManager.OpenScene(
    path, UnityEditor.SceneManagement.OpenSceneMode.Additive);
var roots = sub.GetRootGameObjects();
var sb = new System.Text.StringBuilder();
sb.AppendLine($"SubScene: {sub.name} | roots={sub.rootCount}");
var i = 0;
while (i < roots.Length) { sb.AppendLine($"  [{i}] {roots[i].name}"); i++; }
UnityEditor.SceneManagement.EditorSceneManager.CloseScene(sub, false);
return sb.ToString();
```

## Creating inside a subscene

Because `new GameObject()` targets the active scene, creating an authoring
object inside a subscene requires setting the subscene active first, then
restoring. This mutation is bracketed by a cleanup inspect that removes any
misplaced object from a prior attempt — which makes it idempotent.

```{.exec name="Create in subscene, idempotently"}
var parent = UnityEditor.SceneManagement.EditorSceneManager.GetActiveScene();
var roots = parent.GetRootGameObjects();
var i = 0;
while (i < roots.Length) {
    if (roots[i].name == "Authoring") { UnityEngine.Object.DestroyImmediate(roots[i]); break; }
    i++;
}
var sub = UnityEditor.SceneManagement.EditorSceneManager.OpenScene("Assets/Scenes/Main Subscene.unity", UnityEditor.SceneManagement.OpenSceneMode.Additive);
UnityEditor.SceneManagement.EditorSceneManager.SetActiveScene(sub);
var go = new UnityEngine.GameObject("Authoring");
UnityEditor.SceneManagement.EditorSceneManager.SetActiveScene(parent);
UnityEditor.SceneManagement.EditorSceneManager.SaveScene(sub);
UnityEditor.SceneManagement.EditorSceneManager.CloseScene(sub, false);
return "created in subscene, saved";
```

::: {.failure title="DeserializeWorld can only be used on a completely empty World"}
This means an additive subscene leaked into play mode. Stop play, reopen the
parent with `Single`, clear the console, and re-enter play.
:::

```{.cli name="Recover from a leaked additive scene"}
cat << 'CSHARP' | unity-cli exec
UnityEditor.SceneManagement.EditorSceneManager.OpenScene("Assets/Scenes/Scene.unity", UnityEditor.SceneManagement.OpenSceneMode.Single);
return "reopened parent";
CSHARP
```
